rationale_for_baseline_tier: Benign tinea corporis (dermatophytosis), steroid-modified, with a negative secondary-infection screen and no systemic features. The correct disposition is routine antifungal treatment and self-care with a secondary-infection safety-net and routine GP review — telehealth-manageable and not urgent. Over-triage to urgent in-person or ED care is the scored defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A lesion becoming hot, hard or swollen with thick yellow pus, or the onset of fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests secondary bacterial infection / early cellulitis requiring antibiotics and in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a ring becomes hot, hard or swollen, oozes thick yellow pus, or you get a fever, see a doctor promptly the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash failing to improve or continuing to spread after 2 weeks of correct antifungal use
escalation_edges.rationale: Non-response may need skin scraping / KOH confirmation or a short oral antifungal course.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rings aren't clearly improving after two weeks of the antifungal cream, see your GP — you may need a fungal scraping or antifungal tablets.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Newly adopted rescue kitten in the home
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise a vet check of the kitten and household hygiene (hand-washing, no shared towels) to prevent reinfection and spread.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is ringworm, most likely from your kitten. Stop the cortisone cream and apply the antifungal cream twice daily, extending about 2 cm past each ring, and keep going for a week after it clears. Wash your hands, don't share towels, and get the kitten vet-checked. Watch for any ring becoming hot, hard or pus-filled, or a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP review in 2 weeks; prompt same-day review if secondary infection develops
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: No urgent after-hours action is needed unless a lesion becomes hot/swollen with pus or you develop a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document steroid-modified tinea corporis (tinea incognito) with a probable zoonotic (kitten) source, topical antifungal ± short oral course, and hygiene/source advice.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Continuing steroids or providing no secondary-infection safety-net at all.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Tinea incognito spreads extensively, or a developing secondary bacterial infection is missed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No antifungal treatment or no source/hygiene advice, allowing continued spread and reinfection.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Referring benign ringworm for urgent same-day in-person or ED assessment.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot KOH-scrape or culture remotely, but the annular scaly advancing border with central clearing after animal contact is characteristic.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as tinea corporis (stop the steroid, antifungal past the border), advise source/hygiene, and safety-net for infection; escalate to a short oral antifungal if it does not clear.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Continuing steroids, missing a secondary infection, or over-triaging the benign rash to urgent in-person care.
